subject: Physics
q: In a cuboid of dimension <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>L</mi><mo>×</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>L</mi><mo>×</mo><mi>L</mi></math> , a charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>q</mi></math> is placed at the centre of the surface <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math> having area of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><msup><mi>L</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> . The flux through the opposite surface to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math> is given by
question: In a cuboid of dimension <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>L</mi><mo>×</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>L</mi><mo>×</mo><mi>L</mi></math> , a charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>q</mi></math> is placed at the centre of the surface <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math> having area of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><msup><mi>L</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> . The flux through the opposite surface to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>S</mi></math> is given by
solution:
Assume a similar cuboid on top of the given cuboid to make symmetry about charge. Thus, it becomes a cube of length 2L.

Flux passing through whole cube is ϕ=q∈0(Gauss's Law).

Since, total faces are 6, so flux passing through shaded face =q6∈0.

subject: Physics
q: A point charge $q=1 \mu \mathrm{C}$ is located at a distance 2 cm from one end of a thin insulating wire of length 10 cm having a charge $Q=24 \mu \mathrm{C}$, distributed uniformly along its length, as shown in figure. Force between $q$ and wire is $\_\_\_\_$ N. (Use : $\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{\mathrm{o}}}=9 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{~N}. \mathrm{m}^{2} / \mathrm{C}^{2}$)
question: A point charge $q=1 \mu \mathrm{C}$ is located at a distance 2 cm from one end of a thin insulating wire of length 10 cm having a charge $Q=24 \mu \mathrm{C}$, distributed uniformly along its length, as shown in figure. Force between $q$ and wire is $\_\_\_\_$ N. (Use : $\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{\mathrm{o}}}=9 \times 10^{9} \mathrm{~N}. \mathrm{m}^{2} / \mathrm{C}^{2}$)
solution:
Consider a small element of length $dx$ on the wire at a distance $x$ from the point charge $q$.
The linear charge density of the wire is $\lambda = \frac{Q}{L} = \frac{24 \times 10^{-6}}{0.1} = 240 \times 10^{-6}$ C/m.
The charge on the small element is $dQ = \lambda dx$.
The electrostatic force between the point charge $q$ and the element $dQ$ is $dF = \frac{k q dQ}{x^2} = \frac{k q \lambda dx}{x^2}$.
The total force $F$ is obtained by integrating $dF$ from $x = a$ to $x = a + L$, where $a = 2$ cm and $L = 10$ cm.
$F = \int_{0.02}^{0.12} \frac{k q \lambda}{x^2} dx = k q \lambda \left[ -\frac{1}{x} \right]_{0.02}^{0.12}$
$F = k q \lambda \left( \frac{1}{0.02} - \frac{1}{0.12} \right) = k q \frac{Q}{L} \left( \frac{0.12 - 0.02}{0.02 \times 0.12} \right)$
$F = \frac{k q Q}{L} \frac{L}{a(a+L)} = \frac{k q Q}{a(a+L)}$
Substituting the given values: $k = 9 \times 10^9$ N.m$^2$/C$^2$, $q = 1 \times 10^{-6}$ C, $Q = 24 \times 10^{-6}$ C, $a = 0.02$ m, and $a+L = 0.12$ m.
$F = \frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 1 \times 10^{-6} \times 24 \times 10^{-6}}{0.02 \times 0.12}$
$F = \frac{9 \times 24 \times 10^{-3}}{0.0024} = \frac{216 \times 10^{-3}}{2.4 \times 10^{-3}}$
$F = \frac{216}{2.4} = 90$ N.

q:
A particle of charge -q and mass m moves in a circle of radius r around an infinitely long line charge of linear density +λ. Then time period will be given as:
(Consider k as Coulomb's constant)
question:
A particle of charge -q and mass m moves in a circle of radius r around an infinitely long line charge of linear density +λ. Then time period will be given as:
(Consider k as Coulomb's constant)
solution:
Attractive electrostatic force due to line charge on the charged particle will provide the required centripetal force. Therefore,

q2kλr=mω2r

⇒ω2=2kλqmr2

⇒2πT2=2kλqmr2

⇒T=2πrm2kλq

q:
Two electrons each are fixed at a distance 2d. A third charge proton placed at the midpoint is displaced slightly by a distance xx≪d perpendicular to the line joining the two fixed charges. Proton will execute simple harmonic motion having angular frequency:
(m= mass of charged particle)
question:
Two electrons each are fixed at a distance 2d. A third charge proton placed at the midpoint is displaced slightly by a distance xx≪d perpendicular to the line joining the two fixed charges. Proton will execute simple harmonic motion having angular frequency:
(m= mass of charged particle)
solution:
From the given condition, we have

Fnet q=-2Fq/qcosθ

Fnet q=-2·14πε0·q2d2+x22·xd2+x2

=-q22πε0xd2+x23/2

For x<<d,

Fnet q=-q22πε0 d3x

∴  a=-q22πε0·md3x

Comparing with equation of SHM a=-ω2x

∴  ω=q22πε0md3

subject: Physics
q: A liquid drop having 6 excess electrons is kept stationary under a uniform electric field of $25.5$ $\mathrm{kVm}^{-1}$. The density of liquid is $1.26 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~m}^{-3}$. The radius of the drop is (neglect buoyancy).
question: A liquid drop having 6 excess electrons is kept stationary under a uniform electric field of $25.5$ $\mathrm{kVm}^{-1}$. The density of liquid is $1.26 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~m}^{-3}$. The radius of the drop is (neglect buoyancy).
solution:
$\mathrm{F}=\mathrm{qE}=m g\left(\mathrm{q}=6 \mathrm{e}=6 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right)$
$\operatorname{Density}(\mathrm{d})=\frac{\text { mass }}{\text { volume }}=\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\frac{4}{3} \pi \mathrm{r}^3}$
or $r^3=\frac{m}{\frac{4}{3} \pi d}$
Putting the value of $d$ and $m\left(=\frac{q E}{g}\right)$ and
solving we get $\mathrm{r}=7.8 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~m}$

subject: Physics
q: An electric field $\vec{E}=(2 x \hat{i}) N C^{-1}$ exists in space. A cube of side $2 \mathrm{~m}$ is placed in the space as per figure given below. The electric flux through the cube is ______ $\mathrm{Nm}^2 / \mathrm{C}$.
question: An electric field $\vec{E}=(2 x \hat{i}) N C^{-1}$ exists in space. A cube of side $2 \mathrm{~m}$ is placed in the space as per figure given below. The electric flux through the cube is ______ $\mathrm{Nm}^2 / \mathrm{C}$.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}=2 x \hat{\mathrm{i}} \\ & \phi=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{A}}\end{aligned}$ $\begin{aligned} & \phi_{\text {in }}=-4 \times 4=-16 \mathrm{Nm}^2 / \mathrm{c} \\ & \phi_{\text {out }}=8 \times 4=32 \mathrm{Nm}^2 / \mathrm{c} \\ & d_{\text {net }}=\phi_{\text {in }+} \phi_{\text {out }}=-16+32=16 \mathrm{Nm}^2 / \mathrm{c}\end{aligned}$

solution:

subject: Physics
q:
The electrostatic potential in a charged spherical region of radius $r$ varies as $V=a r^{3}+b$, where $a$ and $b$ are constants. The total charge in the sphere of unit radius is $\alpha \times \pi a \in_{\mathrm{o}}$. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
(permittivity of vacuum is $\epsilon_{0}$)
question:
The electrostatic potential in a charged spherical region of radius $r$ varies as $V=a r^{3}+b$, where $a$ and $b$ are constants. The total charge in the sphere of unit radius is $\alpha \times \pi a \in_{\mathrm{o}}$. The value of $\alpha$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
(permittivity of vacuum is $\epsilon_{0}$)
solution:
From $V = ar^3 + b$, the electric field is $E = -dV/dr = -3ar^2$.
By Gauss's law:
$E(r) \cdot 4\pi r^2 = Q(r)/\epsilon_0$, so $-3ar^2 \times 4\pi r^2 = Q(r)/\epsilon_0$.
This gives $Q(r) = -12\pi ar^4\epsilon_0$.
At $r = 1$: $Q(1) = -12\pi a\epsilon_0$.
Since $Q(1) = \alpha \times \pi a\epsilon_0$, we have $\alpha = -12$

question: The three charges q2,q and q2 are placed at the corners A,D and C of a square of side a as shown in figure. The magnitude of electric field E at the corner B of the square, is

EA=kq2a2=kq2a2,  EC=kq2a2=kq2a2  and  ED=kq2a2=kq2a2

As we can see from diagram, EC & EA are perpendicular.

Therefore, net electric field will be,

=kq2a2+kq2a22+kq2a22=kq2a2+kq2a2=q4πϵ0a212+12

subject: Physics
q: A thin semi-circular ring of radius $r$ has a positive charge $q$ distributed uniformly over it. The net field $\vec{E}$ at the centre $O$ is
question: A thin semi-circular ring of radius $r$ has a positive charge $q$ distributed uniformly over it. The net field $\vec{E}$ at the centre $O$ is
solution: Linear charge density $\lambda=\left(\frac{q}{\pi r}\right)$ $E=\int d E \sin \theta(-\hat{\mathrm{j}})=\int \frac{\mathrm{K} \cdot d q}{\mathrm{r}^2} \sin \theta(-\hat{\mathrm{j}})$ $\mathrm{E}=\frac{\mathrm{K}}{\mathrm{r}^2} \int \frac{\mathrm{qr}}{\pi r} \mathrm{~d} \theta \sin \theta(-\hat{\mathrm{j}})$ $=\frac{\mathrm{K}}{\mathrm{r}^2} \frac{\mathrm{q}}{\pi} \int_0^\pi \sin \theta(-\hat{\mathrm{j}})$ $=\frac{\mathrm{q}}{2 \pi^2 \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{r}^2}(-\hat{\mathrm{j}})$

subject: Physics
q: An electric field of $1000 \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m}$ is applied to an electric dipole at angle of $45^{\circ}$. The value of electric dipole moment is $10^{-29} \mathrm{C} \cdot \mathrm{m} .$ What is the potential energy of the electric dipole?
question: An electric field of $1000 \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m}$ is applied to an electric dipole at angle of $45^{\circ}$. The value of electric dipole moment is $10^{-29} \mathrm{C} \cdot \mathrm{m} .$ What is the potential energy of the electric dipole?
solution:
Potential energy of a dipole is given by $\mathrm{U}=-\overrightarrow{\mathrm{P}} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}$

$=-P E \cos \theta$

[Where $\theta=$ angle between dipole and perpendicular to the field $=-\left(10^{-29}\right)\left(10^{3}\right) \cos 45^{\circ}$

$=-0.707 \times 10^{-26} \mathrm{~J}=-7 \times 10^{-27} \mathrm{~J}$

subject: Physics
q: In finding the electric field using Gauss law the formula <math><mfenced close="|" open="|" separators="|"><mrow><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>q</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi></mrow></msub></mrow><mrow><msub><mrow><mo>ε</mo></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mfenced close="|" open="|" separators="|"><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfrac></math> is applicable. In the formula <math><msub><mrow><mo>ε</mo></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></math> is permittivity of free space, <math><mi>A</mi></math> is the area of Gaussian surface and <math><msub><mrow><mi>q</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi></mrow></msub></math> is charge enclosed by the Gaussian surface. This equation can be used in which of the following situation?
question: In finding the electric field using Gauss law the formula <math><mfenced close="|" open="|" separators="|"><mrow><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><msub><mrow><mi>q</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi></mrow></msub></mrow><mrow><msub><mrow><mo>ε</mo></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mfenced close="|" open="|" separators="|"><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfrac></math> is applicable. In the formula <math><msub><mrow><mo>ε</mo></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub></math> is permittivity of free space, <math><mi>A</mi></math> is the area of Gaussian surface and <math><msub><mrow><mi>q</mi></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi></mrow></msub></math> is charge enclosed by the Gaussian surface. This equation can be used in which of the following situation?
options: Only when the Gaussian surface is an equipotential surface

Only when the Gaussian surface is an equipotential surface and <math><mfenced close="|" open="|" separators="|"><mrow><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced></math> is constant on the surface.
Only when <math><mfenced close="|" open="|" separators="|"><mrow><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>E</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo></math> constant on the surface.
For any choice of Gaussian surface.
solution:
According to Gauss law of electrostatics, the electric flux through an enclosed surface is given by ϕE=qencε0

From the formula of electric flux, ϕ=E→·ds→

Also, the relation between electric field and electric potential is given by V=E→·dr→

So, the formula E→=qencε0A is only applicable when the Gaussian surface is an equipotential surface and E→ is constant on the surface.

subject: Physics
q: A point charge +q is placed at the origin. A second point charge +9 q is placed at $(\mathrm{d}, 0,0)$ in Cartesian coordinate system. The point in between them where the electric field vanishes is :
question: A point charge +q is placed at the origin. A second point charge +9 q is placed at $(\mathrm{d}, 0,0)$ in Cartesian coordinate system. The point in between them where the electric field vanishes is :
solution:
Let $\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{p}}=0$
$\begin{aligned}
& \therefore \frac{\mathrm{kq}}{\mathrm{x}^2}=\frac{\mathrm{k} 9 \mathrm{q}}{(\mathrm{~d}-\mathrm{x})^2} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{~d}-\mathrm{x}}{\mathrm{x}}=3 \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\frac{\mathrm{d}}{4}
\end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ co-ordinate of P is $\left(\frac{\mathrm{d}}{4}, 0,0\right)$

subject: Physics
q: Let σ be the uniform surface charge density of two infinite thin plane sheets shown in figure. Then the electric fields in three different region EI , EII and EIII
question: Let σ be the uniform surface charge density of two infinite thin plane sheets shown in figure. Then the electric fields in three different region EI , EII and EIII
solution:
Electric field due to charged sheet for charge density +σ is E→=+σ2∈0 and for -σ is E→=-σ2∈0.

Assuming RHS to be n^

E→I=σ2∈0-n^+σ2∈0-n^=-σ∈0n^  (leftwards)

E→II=σ2∈0-n^+σ2∈0n^=0

E→III=σ2∈0n^+σ2∈0n^=σ∈0n^    (rightwards)

subject: Physics
q: Two identical conducting spheres $P$ and $S$ with charge $Q$ on each, repel each other with a force $16 \mathrm{~N}$. A third identical uncharged conducting sphere $R$ is successively brought in contact with the two spheres. The new force of repulsion between $\mathrm{P}$ and $\mathrm{S}$ is :
question: Two identical conducting spheres $P$ and $S$ with charge $Q$ on each, repel each other with a force $16 \mathrm{~N}$. A third identical uncharged conducting sphere $R$ is successively brought in contact with the two spheres. The new force of repulsion between $\mathrm{P}$ and $\mathrm{S}$ is :
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & F_{P S} \propto Q^2 \\ & F_{P S}=16 \mathrm{~N} \end{aligned}$
Now If $\mathrm{P} ~\&~ \mathrm{R}$ are brought in contact then
Now If $S \& R$ are brought in contact then
New force between $P ~\&~ S$ is : $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{PS}} \propto \frac{\mathrm{Q}}{2} \times \frac{3 \mathrm{Q}}{4} \\ & \mathrm{~F}_{\mathrm{PS}} \propto \frac{3 \mathrm{Q}^2}{8}=\frac{3}{8} \times 16=6 \mathrm{~N} \end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: Two large plane parallel conducting plates are kept 10 cm apart as shown in figure. The potential difference between them is $V$. The potential difference between the points A and B (shown in the figure) is :
question: Two large plane parallel conducting plates are kept 10 cm apart as shown in figure. The potential difference between them is $V$. The potential difference between the points A and B (shown in the figure) is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { Using } \Delta V=E(\Delta d) \\ & V=E(10) \\ & V_{A B}=E \cdot 4=\frac{V}{10} \times 4=\frac{2 V}{5}\end{aligned}$

q: Find the electric field at point P (as shown in figure) on the perpendicular bisector of a uniformly charged thin wire of length L carrying a charge Q. The distance of the point P from the centre of the rod is a=32L
question: Find the electric field at point P (as shown in figure) on the perpendicular bisector of a uniformly charged thin wire of length L carrying a charge Q. The distance of the point P from the centre of the rod is a=32L
solution:
E=kλasinθ1+sinθ2

E=14πε0×QL×13 L2×2sinθ

tanθ=L/23 L2=13

sinθ=12

E=14πε0×2Q3 L2×2×12

E=Q23πε0 L2

subject: Physics
q: Two point charges $-4 \mu c$ and $4 \mu c$, constituting an electric dipole, are placed at $(-9,0,0) \mathrm{cm}$ and $(9,0,0) \mathrm{cm}$ in a uniform electric field of strength $10^4 \mathrm{NC}^{-1}$. The work done on the dipole in rotating it from the equilibrium through $180^{\circ}$ is :
question: Two point charges $-4 \mu c$ and $4 \mu c$, constituting an electric dipole, are placed at $(-9,0,0) \mathrm{cm}$ and $(9,0,0) \mathrm{cm}$ in a uniform electric field of strength $10^4 \mathrm{NC}^{-1}$. The work done on the dipole in rotating it from the equilibrium through $180^{\circ}$ is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{U}=-\mathrm{PE} \cos \theta \\ & \mathrm{w}_{\mathrm{ext}}=\Delta \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{f}}-\mathrm{U}_{\mathrm{i}}=-\mathrm{PE} \cos 180^{\circ}+\mathrm{PE} \cos 0^{\circ} \\ & \mathrm{w}_{\mathrm{ext}}=2 \mathrm{PE} \\ & =2 \times\left(4 \times 10^{-6}\right)(18) \times 10^4 \\ & =144 \times 10^{-2} \\ & =14.4 \mathrm{~mJ}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: Two uniformly charged spherical conductors <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> of radii <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> are separated by a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. If the spheres are connected by a conducting wire, then in equilibrium condition, the ratio of the magnitudes of the electric fields at the surface of the sphere <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> will be
question: Two uniformly charged spherical conductors <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> of radii <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> are separated by a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. If the spheres are connected by a conducting wire, then in equilibrium condition, the ratio of the magnitudes of the electric fields at the surface of the sphere <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> will be
solution:
When two conductors are connected by a conducting wire, their potentials become equal. Therefore, VA=VB

The potential of a spherical conductor is given by, V=kQR

⇒kQARA=kQBRB

⇒Q∝R

Now the electric field near the surface of the conductor will be, E=kQR2

⇒EAEB=kQARA2kQBRB2=RBRA

⇒EAEB=105=21

=A→1=0.2 m2i^

Now, ϕa=3E05i^+4E05j^·0.2i^=3×0.25E0

& ϕb=3E05i^+4E05j^·0.3j^=4×0.35E0

Now, ϕaϕb=0.61.2=12=ab

⇒a:b=1:2

⇒a=1

subject: Physics
q:
Choose the incorrect statement:
(a) The electric lines of force entering into a Gaussian surface provide negative flux.
(b) A charge q is placed at the centre of a cube. The flux through all the faces will be the same.
(c) In a uniform electric field net flux through a closed Gaussian surface containing no net charge, is zero.
(d) When an electric field is parallel to a Gaussian surface, it provides a finite non-zero flux.

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
question:
Choose the incorrect statement:
(a) The electric lines of force entering into a Gaussian surface provide negative flux.
(b) A charge q is placed at the centre of a cube. The flux through all the faces will be the same.
(c) In a uniform electric field net flux through a closed Gaussian surface containing no net charge, is zero.
(d) When an electric field is parallel to a Gaussian surface, it provides a finite non-zero flux.

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
solution:
We know that electric flux is a scalar quantity and this is the scalar product of the magnitude of electric field and surface area. 

Recall, the formula given by Gauss theorem, ϕ=E→·A→=EAcosθ, here θ=90°, it means, ϕ=0.

question: Electric charge is transferred to an irregular metallic disk as shown in figure. If $\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \sigma_3$ and $\sigma_4$ are charge densities at given points then, choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \sigma \propto \frac{1}{\mathrm{ROC}} \\ & (\mathrm{ROC})_1 \lt (\mathrm{ROC})_3 \lt (\mathrm{ROC})_2=(\mathrm{ROC})_4 \\ & \sigma_1 \gt \sigma_3 \gt \sigma_2=\sigma_4\end{aligned}$

 The force between the dipoles is :
question:
Two point dipoles of dipole moment $\vec{p}_1$ and $\vec{p}_2$ are at a distance $x$ from each other and $\vec{p}_1 || \vec{p}_2$. 
 The force between the dipoles is :
solution: Force of interaction $\mathrm{F}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \cdot \frac{3 \mathrm{p}_1 \mathrm{p}_2}{\mathrm{r}^4}$

subject: Physics
q: In the first configuration (1) as shown in the figure, four identical charges \(\left(q_0\right)\) are kept at the corners \(A, B, C\) and \(D\) of square of side length 'a'. In the second configuration (2), the same charges are shifted to mid points \(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{E}, \mathrm{H}\) and F, of the square, If \(\mathrm{K}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\), the difference between the potential energies of configuration (2) and (1) is given by :
question: In the first configuration (1) as shown in the figure, four identical charges \(\left(q_0\right)\) are kept at the corners \(A, B, C\) and \(D\) of square of side length 'a'. In the second configuration (2), the same charges are shifted to mid points \(\mathrm{G}, \mathrm{E}, \mathrm{H}\) and F, of the square, If \(\mathrm{K}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\), the difference between the potential energies of configuration (2) and (1) is given by :
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& u_{\oplus}=\left(2 \frac{K q_0}{a}+\frac{K q_0}{\sqrt{2} a}\right) q_0 \times 2 \\
& u_0=\left(2 \frac{K q_0 \sqrt{2}}{a}+\frac{K q_0}{a}\right) q_0 \times 2
\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \text { So, } \quad \Delta u=u_2-u_1=2 q_0 \frac{k q_0}{a}\left[2 \sqrt{2}+1-2-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right] \\
& \Rightarrow \quad \Delta u=\frac{2 q_0^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 a}\left[\frac{4-\sqrt{2}-1}{\sqrt{2}}\right]=\frac{2 q_0^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 a} \frac{(3-\sqrt{2})}{\sqrt{2}} \\
& \Rightarrow \quad \Delta u=\frac{2 k q_0^2}{a}\left[\frac{3-\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{2}}\right]=\frac{k q_0^2}{a}(3 \sqrt{2}-2)
\end{aligned}$

q:
A cubical volume is bounded by the surfaces x=0, x=a, y=0, y=a, z=0, z=a. The electric field in the region is given by E→=E0xi^. Where E0=4×104 NC-1 m-1. If a=2 cm, the charge contained in the cubical volume is Q×10–14 C. The value of Q is ______.

(Take ϵ0=9×10-12 C2 N-1m-2)
question:
A cubical volume is bounded by the surfaces x=0, x=a, y=0, y=a, z=0, z=a. The electric field in the region is given by E→=E0xi^. Where E0=4×104 NC-1 m-1. If a=2 cm, the charge contained in the cubical volume is Q×10–14 C. The value of Q is ______.

(Take ϵ0=9×10-12 C2 N-1m-2)
solution:
Given here, electric field, E→=E0xi^

Here, the flux passes mainly through surface areas, ABCD and EFGH. As the surfaces AEFB and CGHD are parallel to the electric field, so flux for these surfaces are zero. Again in EFGH, a=0, thus, electric field is zero.

Hence, the flux only passes through the surface are ABCD.

The net flux is ϕnet=ϕABCD=E0a·a2

Using Gauss's law, qenϵ0=E0a3

⇒qen=E0ε0a3

=4×104×9×10-12×8×10-6

=288×10-14 C

Hence, the value of Q=288.

q: The magnitude of the average electric field normally present in the atmosphere just above the surface of the Earth is about <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>150</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo>/</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>, directed inward towards the center of the Earth. This gives the total net surface charge carried by the Earth to be : [Given : <math> <mrow> <msub> <mo>∈</mo> <mtext>O</mtext> </msub> <mo>=</mo> <mtext>8.85</mtext> <mo>×</mo> <mn>1</mn> <msup> <mn>0</mn> <mrow> <mo>-</mo> <mn>1</mn> <mn>2</mn> </mrow> </msup> <msup> <mtext>  C</mtext> <mn>2</mn> </msup> <mo>/</mo> <msup> <mtext>N-m</mtext> <mn>2</mn> </msup> <mtext>,  </mtext> <msub> <mtext>R</mtext> <mtext>E</mtext> </msub> <mo>=</mo> <mtext>6.37</mtext> <mo>×</mo> <mn>1</mn> <msup> <mn>0</mn> <mn>6</mn> </msup> <mtext>m</mtext> </mrow> </math>]
question: The magnitude of the average electric field normally present in the atmosphere just above the surface of the Earth is about <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>150</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo>/</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>, directed inward towards the center of the Earth. This gives the total net surface charge carried by the Earth to be : [Given : <math> <mrow> <msub> <mo>∈</mo> <mtext>O</mtext> </msub> <mo>=</mo> <mtext>8.85</mtext> <mo>×</mo> <mn>1</mn> <msup> <mn>0</mn> <mrow> <mo>-</mo> <mn>1</mn> <mn>2</mn> </mrow> </msup> <msup> <mtext>  C</mtext> <mn>2</mn> </msup> <mo>/</mo> <msup> <mtext>N-m</mtext> <mn>2</mn> </msup> <mtext>,  </mtext> <msub> <mtext>R</mtext> <mtext>E</mtext> </msub> <mo>=</mo> <mtext>6.37</mtext> <mo>×</mo> <mn>1</mn> <msup> <mn>0</mn> <mn>6</mn> </msup> <mtext>m</mtext> </mrow> </math>]
solution:
E=150 N/C

We know that  ϕ = E → · A →

                          = E × 4 π r 2

                          = 1 5 0 × 4 π × R e 2

Now, ϕ = Q in ∈ o





Q in = ϕ ∈ o = 1 5 0 × 4 π R e 2 × 8.85 × 1 0 - 1 2  C

      = 1 5 0 × 4 π 6.37 × 1 0 6 2 × 8.85 × 1 0 - 1 2

      = 6 0 0 π × 6.37 × 6.37 × 8.85 C

      =  6 8 0  kC

As Electric field is inward, Q will be negative

subject: Physics
q: A thin half ring of radius $35$ cm is uniformly charged with a total charge of $Q$ coulomb. If the magnitude of the electric field at centre of the half ring is $100$ V/m, then the value of $Q$ is _______ nC. ($\epsilon_o = 8.85 \times 10^{-12}$ C$^2$/Nm$^2$ and $\pi = 3.14$)
question: A thin half ring of radius $35$ cm is uniformly charged with a total charge of $Q$ coulomb. If the magnitude of the electric field at centre of the half ring is $100$ V/m, then the value of $Q$ is _______ nC. ($\epsilon_o = 8.85 \times 10^{-12}$ C$^2$/Nm$^2$ and $\pi = 3.14$)
solution:
The electric field at the center of a uniformly charged half ring is given by:

$E = \dfrac{2k\lambda}{R}$

where $\lambda = \dfrac{Q}{\pi R}$ is the linear charge density.

Substituting $\lambda$, we get:

$E = \dfrac{2kQ}{\pi R^2} = \dfrac{2Q}{4\pi\epsilon_0 \pi R^2} = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi^2\epsilon_0 R^2}$

Rearranging for $Q$:

$Q = 2\pi^2\epsilon_0 R^2 E$

Given values:
$E = 100$ V/m
$R = 35$ cm $= 0.35$ m
$\epsilon_0 = 8.85 \times 10^{-12}$ C$^2$/Nm$^2$
$\pi = 3.14$

Substituting the values:

$Q = 2 \times (3.14)^2 \times 8.85 \times 10^{-12} \times (0.35)^2 \times 100$

$Q = 2 \times 9.8596 \times 8.85 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.1225 \times 100$

$Q \approx 2137.8 \times 10^{-12}$ C

$Q \approx 2.14 \times 10^{-9}$ C $= 2.14$ nC

Answer: $2.14$

subject: Physics
q:
For a charged spherical ball, electrostatic potential inside the ball varies with r as V=2ar2+b.

Here, a and b are constant and r is the distance from the center. The volume charge density inside the ball is -λaε. The value of λ is ______.

ε= permittivity of medium.
question:
For a charged spherical ball, electrostatic potential inside the ball varies with r as V=2ar2+b.

Here, a and b are constant and r is the distance from the center. The volume charge density inside the ball is -λaε. The value of λ is ______.

ε= permittivity of medium.
solution:
As we know, E=-dVdr=-4ar

Now, electric field inside the charged sphere is given by E=ρr3ε. 

Comparing both, we get ρ=-12aε.

Therefore, λ=12.
